figures.gloss: The elderly, supreme creator deity who rules the heavens and day. He is the first priest, the inventor of writing, the calendar, and agriculture, guiding humanity through esoteric cosmic mathematics rather than raw physical force.
figures.facet: Unlike thunder-hurling sky-fathers, he asserts that the universe is governed by profound calculation and ritual exactitude. He represents the scholarly, priestly ideal of Maya kingship, controlling creation through literacy and timekeeping.
figures.provenance: Diego de Landa, 'Relación de las cosas de Yucatán' (1566); Madrid Codex; Dresden Codex.

figures.gloss: The cunning, ball-playing brothers who descend into the underworld to avenge their murdered father. Surviving Xibalba's trap-filled houses, they defeat the Lords of Death, ascending to become the sun and moon.
figures.facet: They conquer death not by overpowering it, but by outperforming it. They voluntarily submit to the underworld's sacrificial oven, using their subsequent resurrection as an elaborate magic trick to coax the Death Lords into begging to be sacrificed themselves.
figures.provenance: Francisco Ximénez, translation of the 'Popol Vuh' (~1701); Classic Maya ceramic vessel iconography.

figures.gloss: The rulers of the subterranean Place of Fright, commanding lesser demons of disease, pus, and jaundice. They challenge noisy surface-dwellers to the Mesoamerican ballgame to defeat, sacrifice, and humiliate them.
figures.facet: They are entirely stripped of the solemn majesty usually afforded to death gods. They are vain, petty aristocratic bullies who cheat at sports—framing mortality not as cosmic justice, but as a predatory hazard that must be outsmarted.

figures.gloss: The fierce, cross-eyed deity of the daytime sun. He requires a constant diet of royal blood and captive sacrifice to sustain his burning heat as he traverses the sky.
figures.facet: The sun is deeply predatory and inherently dual. At sunset, he transforms into the Jaguar God of the Underworld, fighting his way through the subterranean dark each night. His light is literally fueled by continuous warfare.
figures.provenance: Dresden Codex; Temple of the Sun inscriptions at Palenque; monumental stucco masks.

figures.gloss: The reptilian, goggle-eyed lord of storms who strikes the clouds with his lightning axe to release rain. He is simultaneously one entity and four distinct directional gods (the Chaacob) colored by the world's corners.
figures.facet: He physically embodies the fractal, chaotic nature of weather in the Yucatán. He must be constantly placated to prevent him from holding back life-giving rain or unleashing crop-destroying tempests, keeping humanity on a razor's edge of survival.
figures.provenance: Dresden Codex; Postclassic architectural masks at Uxmal and Chichen Itza.

figures.gloss: The creator deity who moves between the earth and the sky, commanding wind and water. He helped form humanity from maize and serves as the ultimate conduit between the human and divine realms.
figures.facet: In the Maya sphere, the feathered serpent is intimately tied to the 'vision serpent'—the actual physical manifestation of the ancestor or god that emerges from the smoke of burning, blood-soaked bark paper during royal bloodletting.
figures.provenance: Diego de Landa, 'Relación de las cosas de Yucatán'; 'Chilam Balam of Chumayel'; 'Popol Vuh'.

figures.gloss: The multifaceted goddess of the moon, weaving, midwifery, medicine, and catastrophic floods. She is often depicted as an elderly woman emptying a jug of water onto the earth.
figures.facet: She perfectly embodies the destructive paradox of water. She is the tender midwife of childbirth and the weaver of life, but simultaneously the apocalyptic crone who pours out the rotting, cataclysmic floods that end world ages.
figures.provenance: Dresden Codex; accounts of shrines at Cozumel and Isla Mujeres by early Spanish explorers.

figures.gloss: The colossal ceiba tree forming the central axis of the cosmos, its roots plunging deep into Xibalba and its branches holding up the thirteen levels of the heavens.
figures.facet: It is not a static geographic feature, but an active, temporary ritual conduit. Maya kings physically *become* the tree during bloodletting ceremonies, making the human sovereign the literal structural pillar holding reality together.
figures.provenance: Sarcophagus lid of K'inich Janaab Pakal (683 CE); San Bartolo murals.

figures.gloss: The animating, sacred life force that circulates through the cosmos, heavily concentrated in human blood, tree sap, incense smoke, and the breath of deities.
figures.facet: The Maya concept of the sacred is intensely corporeal. Divinity is a measurable, communicable liquid that must be physically extracted from humans and fed to the gods, who will literally starve and collapse without this caloric spiritual energy.
figures.provenance: David Stuart, 'The Blood of Kings' (1986); Stephen Houston, 'The Life Within' (2014).

edges.note: Center-tree and center-mountain: the same quincunx of four quarters around a holy middle.
Tree at the center, four directions / four rivers running to the quarters of the world.
The Twins win their lives by out-playing death at the court — the founding match of the Popol Vuh.
Life against death across the same court — the cosmic contest with bodies as the pieces.
Quincunx: four directions around a green center — the board and the world-tree are the same diagram.
Four quarters around a green center — the directional gods and the world-tree are one diagram.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The profound elevation of a dyad; a spiritual pairing necessary to navigate the severe trials of the cosmos and approach the sacred.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Operating as a brotherly dyad (with John Frum) that outwits the lords of the current world order to secure survival.
GOLD SEAM: In the Americas, indigenous populations and missionaries frequently synthesized the Christian cross directly with the Mesoamerican axis mundi.
A cosmic tree joining the worlds along the central axis — the Batang Garing rhymes with the Maya ceiba as world-axis.
Divine twins who pass through death to set the world right — but the Dogon twins are watery, androgynous redeemers where the Maya twins are hunters who outwit death.

Speculative: both figure the dead as hungry and ungoverned — but the pēy are a swarming appetite, not a court, where Xibalba is a ruled kingdom. A loose rhyme of the dead-as-hunger; flagged for the difference.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A sacred tree anchoring the vertical axis of indigenous cosmologies.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fraternal culture heroes whose cooperative and competitive dynamic establishes the tools and limits of humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Endemic, towering flora acting as the central axis of religious life and the required conduit for ritual and sacrificial offerings.

American divine twins who bring order against death and disorder — Sapling and Flint of the Northeast beside Hunahpu and Xbalanque of the Popol Vuh; the contending or cooperating brother-pair as a New-World cosmogonic engine, no shared line between them.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A central axis mundi that maps the flow of divine forces down into the material world.
GOLD SEAM: Elder deities residing in the subterranean depths, representing a darker, obsolete cosmic era.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A colossal, sacred tree serving as the vertical conduit between the terrestrial and divine realms.
Both set a vertical axis joining the worlds at the centre of a quartered space — but the dikenga's axis is a line of light and water, not a tree. A loose axis-mundi echo, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A colossal, sheltering sacred tree acting as an axis mundi that physically connects the terrestrial realm with the sky above.
Intra-tradition: Often paired as the primordial creator couple of the uppermost heavens.
Intra-tradition: The twin engines of agricultural survival, balancing scorching solar heat with torrential rains.
Intra-tradition: The World Tree acts as the primary vascular structure through which the sacred blood-essence (k'uh) flows between realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct Maya expression of the pan-Mesoamerican feathered serpent creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct Maya equivalent of the Central Mexican storm and rain deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shared Mesoamerican conceptualizations of a flowing, animating sacred energy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Elderly, scholarly gods who invent writing and the calendar to establish cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Creator deities who shape the cosmos through thought, speech, and esoteric knowledge rather than physical combat.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Storm gods who violently strike the heavens with an axe or hammer to produce thunder and lightning.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Martial weather deities essential for releasing the waters needed for agricultural survival.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Solar sovereigns who rule the sky by day and must violently battle their way through the underworld each night.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cunning tricksters who rely on illusion, shape-shifting, and sleight-of-hand to undo their more powerful adversaries.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Subterranean sovereigns commanding the dismal realm where the dead dissolve in darkness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Rulers of a dangerous, trap-laden underworld who strip descending heroes or deities of their power and vitality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fearsome mother goddesses who embody both vital terrestrial creation and terrifying, unyielding destruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The colossal cosmic tree that spans the heavens, the mortal plane, and the underworld, binding the universe together.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cruel, entrenched sovereigns who demand impossible, often lethal tribute from their subjugated populations.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both serve as localized axes mundi connecting the underworld, earth, and heavens for ritual interaction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve celestial, monster-slaying champions who navigate the perilous underworld to secure martial order for the surface.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A dyad of fiercely bonded, unruly brothers who navigate and ultimately disrupt the established order of their civilization.

The Diné Hero Twins and the Maya Hunahpu and Xbalanque are the great American twin-pair — both born of a marvel, both ridding the world of devouring powers — but a structural rhyme across unrelated families, NOT a shared line.
Paired saviour-brothers who overcome monstrous adversaries together; the Diné split into striker and watcher where the Maya twins are two equal tricksters, two faces of one widespread twin-archetype.
GOLD SEAM: The towering natural spire that acts as the unbroken conduit connecting the earth to the divine realm.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Predatory, subterranean or acausal lords who actively scheme to trick, test, and consume human life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A great tree acting as the blood-drenched conduit bridging the living and the dead.

The Pueblo war-twins and the Maya Hunahpu and Xbalanque are paired marvel-born brothers who tame or slay the monstrous and patron games — a structural rhyme of the American divine-twins across unrelated families, not a shared line.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Specific living trees treated as the untouchable, sacred center of the world that connect the earthly and divine planes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A colossal sacred tree functioning as a localized axis mundi and the primary site for ancestral communication.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A vertical axis mundi structurally connecting the underworld, the earthly realm, and the heavens.

Death-rulers of a hidden New-World underworld; the Taíno Coaybay of the navel-less dead and the Maya Xibalba of One Death and Seven Death, kindred in setting though Coaybay neither judges nor plays the killing-game.

American divine twins, but the Antillean pair is split into wet and dry weather rather than the Maya twins' shared adventure against death; a loose rhyme of the twin-archetype on opposite functions, flagged as a reach.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The central vertical pillar linking the cosmic realms, marking the energetic heart and origin of the world.
GOLD SEAM: Both feature a profound dyad who descend into a mortal, perilous realm to conquer death and establish a pathway for human ascension.
GOLD SEAM: The central world-tree serving as the conduit for divine blessing, structurally uniting the heavens and the earth.
GOLD SEAM: Both act as the vital vertical axis structurally connecting the underworld, earth, and heavens.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The massive, sacred central tree acting as the physical and spiritual anchor for cosmic order and community assembly.
GOLD SEAM: The central vertical axis that stabilizes the cosmos and connects the heavenly, earthly, and subterranean realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The pan-Mesoamerican cosmic tree motif, here highly localized as the specific birth-site of the Mixtec royalty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The elite lords of the subterranean death realm, demanding blood sacrifice and hosting the dead kings.

dossier.essence: The religious tradition of the Maya peoples of the Yucatán, Guatemala, Belize, and adjacent Chiapas and Honduras — a civilization whose Classic-period city-states (c. 250–900 CE) produced the most sophisticated writing and calendrics of the pre-Columbian Americas, and whose descendants, some six million Maya across roughly thirty languages, remain a living people. It bound divine kingship, a vast calendar, and a pantheon (Itzamna, K'inich Ajaw, Chaac, the Hero Twins, the Lords of Xibalba) to the conviction that the gods must be sustained by human offering, above all blood.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is layered — conventionally thirteen heavens above and a nine-level underworld (Xibalba) below — organized around a central world-tree (the ceiba, Wacah Chan) and four directional quarters with their colors. Time is immense and cyclical, tracked by the interlocking 260-day (Tzolk'in) and 365-day (Haab') counts and the Long Count; the present world is a repaired creation after earlier failed attempts, humanity finally shaped from maize (Popol Vuh). The sacred is a communicable essence, k'uh, concentrated in blood, sap, and smoke, which the gods require to keep the cosmos in motion.
dossier.practice: Divine kings (k'uhul ajaw) and calendar priests (ah k'in) mediated with the gods through autosacrificial bloodletting, the sacrifice of captives, incense (copal) offering, the ballgame, and precise calendrical ritual and divination. Among living Maya, daykeepers (ajq'ij) still keep the 260-day count, and 'costumbre' blends older practice with Catholicism in the highland cofradía and cargo systems.
dossier.canon: No single scripture: hieroglyphic inscriptions and the surviving bark-paper codices (Dresden, Madrid, Paris), plus colonial-era texts — the K'iche' Popol Vuh, the Yucatec Books of Chilam Balam, and Diego de Landa's Relación de las cosas de Yucatán (1566).
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds sacred time itself — divinity apprehended through an exact, cyclical calendar in which the day is a living force, and the maintenance of the cosmos is the work of precise reckoning and blood-debt.
sources: Dennis Tedlock (trans.), 'Popol Vuh: The Mayan Book of the Dawn of Life' (rev. ed., 1996)
Linda Schele & David Freidel, 'A Forest of Kings: The Untold Story of the Ancient Maya' (1990)
Michael D. Coe & Stephen Houston, 'The Maya' (9th ed., 2015)
